summary: Jeff Atwood highlights a new blog called Spurious Pundit, sharing its insights on managing developers and the fundamental differences between technical and business mindsets. The post explores how managers often fail to communicate implicit expectations when delegating tasks, using a picture-hanging analogy. It then examines how technical work operates in a 'reality-based' universe where things either work or fail based on observable properties, while business success is often self-referentially tied to belief and confidence. Atwood endorses the blog's argument that humility and openness to criticism are essential virtues for technical work, contrasting sharply with the confidence-driven traits valued in sales and management.
key_points: Managers often fail to communicate implicit constraints and expectations when delegating tasks to less experienced developers
Pair programming is an effective way to onboard developers and transfer tacit knowledge
Technical work is 'reality-based' - code either works or fails based on objective properties, unlike business where belief can shape outcomes
The confidence and self-assurance that makes salespeople and managers effective can be detrimental to technical work
Humility, openness to criticism, and valuing people who poke holes in your designs leads to better technical solutions
money_quotes: Technical staff actually operate in a different universe from the business side of the house. They're 'reality-based' in the disparaging way that our current administration uses the term.

Put simply, shit will either work or fail based on the properties of the observable universe. No amount of personal motivation or focus-group work will let a spare, obsolete PC handle a million page hits a day.
On the business side of the house, to an alarming extent, believing things makes them true.
These traits which managers and sales people need to be effective are actually bad for technical folks. Arrogance means you overlook things.
Being able to sell them on it by force of personality doesn't make it better. Being aware of your own failings and limitations makes it better.
Learning to value the people who will poke holes in your designs - the ones who are 'not team players' - makes it better.
key_insight: Technical excellence requires humility and openness to criticism, traits that are often at odds with the confidence-driven mindset rewarded in business and management roles.
